children.title: Anatomy
Orbit & Ocular Adnexa
Orbit
7 bones: frontal, zygomatic, maxilla, palatine, lacrimal, ethmoid, sphenoid
Orbital roof → frontal bone (lacrimal fossa)
Medial wall → thinnest (lamina papyracea)
Floor → prone to blow-out fractures
Lateral wall → strongest
Foramina: optic canal (CN II + ophthalmic artery), superior orbital fissure (CN III/IV/V1/VI), inferior orbital fissure
Periorbital sinuses: frontal, ethmoid, maxillary, sphenoid
Blood supply: ophthalmic artery (branch of ICA)
Extraocular Muscles
Mnemonic: LR6 SO4 All III
4 recti + 2 obliques + levator palpebrae
Origins: annulus of Zinn (recti), trochlea (SO), orbital roof (IO)
Actions: SR (elevation+intorsion), IR (depression+extorsion), MR (adduction), LR (abduction), SO (intorsion+depression), IO (extorsion+elevation)
Diplopia patterns: know which muscle is weak by gaze direction
Ciliary Ganglion
Located in posterior orbit
Short ciliary nerves → pupil sphincter + ciliary muscle (parasympathetic)
3 roots: parasympathetic (CN III), sympathetic, sensory (nasociliary)
Eyelids
Layers (anterior to posterior): skin → orbicularis → orbital septum → fat → levator/tarsus → conjunctiva
Tarsal plate: dense fibrous tissue, gives lid structure
Meibomian glands: sebaceous glands in tarsus → lipid layer of tears
Müller muscle: sympathetic, accessory upper lid retractor
Blood supply: medial & lateral palpebral arteries
Lymphatics: medial → submandibular; lateral → preauricular
Lacrimal System
Lacrimal gland (orbital + palpebral lobes) → accessory glands (Krause, Wolfring)
Drainage: puncta → canaliculi → lacrimal sac → nasolacrimal duct → inferior meatus
Nasolacrimal duct opens under inferior turbinate
Conjunctiva & Related
Bulbar (covers globe) + palpebral (lines lids) + fornix
Goblet cells → mucin layer of tears
Caruncle: modified skin at medial canthus
Plica semilunaris: vestigial third eyelid
Tenon capsule: fibrous envelope around globe (surgical plane for implants)
The Eye
Cornea
5 layers: Epithelium → Bowman → Stroma (90%) → Descemet → Endothelium
Transparent, avascular, highly innervated (CN V1 via nasociliary)
Endothelium: does NOT regenerate; pump-leak mechanism maintains dehydration/clarity
~43D of refracting power (total eye ~60D)
Central thinner (~0.52mm) than periphery (~0.67mm)
Limbus & Sclera
Limbus: corneoscleral junction; site of stem cells for corneal epithelium
Sclera: dense collagen, protects globe; emissary canals for vessels/nerves
Lamina cribrosa: sieve-like scleral region where ON exits (vulnerable in glaucoma)
Anterior Chamber
Bounded by corneal endothelium anteriorly, iris/lens posteriorly
Filled with aqueous humor
Angle structures: Schwalbe line → trabecular meshwork → scleral spur → ciliary band → iris
Trabecular Meshwork & Schlemm Canal
3 layers: uveal → corneoscleral → juxtacanalicular (JCT)
JCT is site of main aqueous resistance
Schlemm canal → collector channels → episcleral veins
Conventional (trabecular) outflow pathway
Uvea
Iris: sphincter (PS, miosis) + dilator (sympathetic, mydriasis); stroma from neural crest
Ciliary body: ciliary muscle (accommodation) + ciliary epithelium (aqueous production)
Choroid: highly vascular; choriocapillaris supplies outer retina; Bruch membrane between RPE and choroid
Lens
Capsule → epithelium → cortex → nucleus
Crystallins: major structural proteins (α, β, γ)
Zonular fibers (from ciliary body) suspend lens
Accommodation: ciliary contraction → zonules relax → lens rounds → more converging power
Avascular; nutrition via aqueous humor
Vitreous
~99% water + collagen type II + hyaluronan
Hyalocytes: vitreous macrophages
Base: strongest vitreoretinal adhesion (ora serrata region)
Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD): floaters + flashes; risk of retinal tear
Retina
10 layers: ILM → NFL → GCL → IPL → INL → OPL → ONL → ELM → photoreceptors → RPE
Rods: ~120M; periphery; night/scotopic vision; rhodopsin
Cones: ~6M; macula/fovea; color/photopic vision; S/M/L opsins
Fovea: only cones, no inner layers (foveal pit); highest acuity
Macula: ~5mm, 2 disc diameters temporal to disc; contains xanthophyll pigment
Optic disc: no photoreceptors = blind spot; NFL converges here
Dual blood supply: central retinal artery (inner) + choriocapillaris via RPE (outer)
Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE)
Monolayer of hexagonal pigmented cells between retina and Bruch membrane
Functions: vitamin A recycling, phagocytosis of photoreceptor outer segments, blood-retina barrier, nutrient transport, secretion (VEGF, PEDF)
Autophagy role in clearing cellular debris
Central role in AMD pathogenesis
Ora Serrata & Bruch Membrane
Ora serrata: anterior termination of retina (3mm nasal, 6mm temporal to limbus)
Bruch membrane: 5 layers; pentalaminar structure; accumulates lipids/drusen in AMD
Neuro-Ophthalmic Anatomy
Optic Nerve (CN II)
4 segments: intraocular (1mm) → intraorbital (25mm) → intracanalicular (9mm) → intracranial (10mm)
Intraorbital ON is longer than orbit → allows eye movement without traction
Blood supply: posterior ciliary arteries (optic disc), ophthalmic artery branches
Visual Pathway & Field Defects
Retina → ON → optic chiasm → optic tract → LGN → optic radiations → V1 (calcarine cortex)
Chiasm: nasal fibers cross; temporal fibers stay ipsilateral
Monocular blindness → optic nerve lesion
Bitemporal hemianopia → chiasm (pituitary tumor)
Homonymous hemianopia → post-chiasmal (tract/radiation/cortex)
Macular sparing → occipital cortex lesion (dual blood supply)
Key Cranial Nerves
CN III (oculomotor): SR/IR/MR/IO + levator + pupil sphincter; palsy = down-and-out + ptosis + mydriasis
CN IV (trochlear): SO; palsy = vertical diplopia worse looking down/inward
CN V (trigeminal): corneal sensation (V1); blink reflex afferent
CN VI (abducens): LR; palsy = esotropia + horizontal diplopia
CN VII (facial): orbicularis + lacrimal reflex efferent; palsy = lagophthalmos
Pupil Pathways
Afferent: CN II → pretectal nucleus → bilateral Edinger-Westphal → CN III → ciliary ganglion → sphincter
RAPD (Marcus Gunn pupil): afferent defect; swinging flashlight test
Horner syndrome: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis (sympathetic chain lesion)
Cerebral Vascular
Circle of Willis: ICA + ACA + MCA + PCA + basilar
Cavernous sinus: CN III, IV, V1, V2, VI pass through; CN VI most medial (vulnerable)
Posterior communicating artery aneurysm → CN III palsy (pupil-involving)
Embryology
Eye Development Timeline
Week 3-4: optic vesicle evaginates from diencephalon
Week 4-5: optic cup forms (double-walled); lens placode invaginates
Week 5-7: embryonic (choroidal) fissure closes; hyaloid vessels present
Week 7: failure of fissure closure → coloboma
Outer cup layer → RPE; inner cup → neurosensory retina
Tissue Origins
Neuroectoderm: retina, RPE, ciliary epithelium, iris epithelium, optic nerve
Surface ectoderm: lens, corneal epithelium, lacrimal gland, conjunctival epithelium
Neural crest: corneal stroma/endothelium, iris stroma, trabecular meshwork, sclera, orbital bones/cartilage, ciliary ganglion
Mesoderm: extraocular muscles, vascular endothelium, vitreous
Genetic Cascades
Homeobox genes (PAX6, SOX2, LHX2): master regulators of eye development
PAX6 mutations → aniridia
Morphogens (Shh, Wnt, FGF): concentration gradients guide cell fate
Congenital Anomalies
Coloboma: failure of fissure closure (inferior-nasal location); affects iris, lens, retina, ON
Microphthalmia: small eye; associated with CHARGE syndrome
Anophthalmia: absent eye
Persistent fetal vasculature (PFV): failed regression of hyaloid artery → leukocoria
Aniridia: PAX6 mutation; associated with Wilms tumor (WAGR)
Genetics
Molecular Genetics
Cell cycle: G1 → S (DNA synthesis) → G2 → M (mitosis); regulated by cyclins/CDKs
DNA repair mechanisms: base excision, nucleotide excision, mismatch repair
Apoptosis: programmed cell death; p53 is key regulator
Epigenetics: methylation + imprinting + X-inactivation (Lyonization)
Alternative splicing → multiple proteins from one gene (isoforms)
Gene therapy: viral vectors (AAV preferred for retina) for recessive disease; antisense/siRNA for dominant
GWAS: genome-wide association studies; useful in AMD, glaucoma
Anticipation: worsening with each generation (e.g., triplet repeat disorders)
Penetrance vs expressivity: penetrance = does it manifest; expressivity = how much
Inheritance Patterns
Autosomal Dominant: 50% risk; one mutant allele sufficient; variable expressivity
Autosomal Recessive: 25% risk; two mutant alleles needed; often enzyme deficiencies
X-linked Recessive: males affected; females carriers (Lyonization → variable expression)
Mitochondrial (maternal): all children of affected mother at risk; LHON, CPEO, MELAS, NARP
Clinical Genetics
Pedigree analysis: identify pattern before testing
Chromosome analysis: FISH, karyotype; aneuploidy (Trisomy 21 → Brushfield spots)
Pharmacogenetics: genetic variation affects drug metabolism (e.g., CYP450)
Genetic counseling: accurate diagnosis → recurrence risk → reproductive options → support resources
High-Yield Ophthalmic Genetics
Retinoblastoma
RB1 gene (13q14); two-hit hypothesis (Knudson)
Hereditary: germline mutation → bilateral, younger onset
Sporadic: somatic mutations → unilateral
Leukocoria + strabismus = key presentation
LHON (Leber Hereditary Optic Neuropathy): mitochondrial; mt-ND genes; acute painless vision loss young males
Retinitis Pigmentosa: AD/AR/X-linked; rhodopsin most common AD gene; night blindness → tunnel vision → eventual blindness
Stargardt disease: ABCA4 (AR); macular dystrophy; 'beaten bronze' macula
Marfan syndrome: FBN1 (AD); lens subluxation (superotemporal)
Homocystinuria: CBS (AR); lens subluxation (inferonasal)
Biochemistry & Physiology
Tear Film
3 layers: lipid (Meibomian glands, prevents evaporation) → aqueous/mucin (lacrimal gland + goblet cells)
Modern model: mucoaqueous layer (mucin gradient) + lipid layer on top
Dry Eye: aqueous-deficient (Sjögren, lacrimal gland dysfunction) vs evaporative (Meibomian gland dysfunction)
DEWS II: chronic inflammatory disease of ocular surface
Cornea
Stroma: collagen type I fibrils in regular orthogonal lamellae → transparency
Endothelium: Na/K-ATPase pump keeps stroma dehydrated; density ~2500 cells/mm²; does NOT regenerate
Epithelium: rapid turnover (~7 days); barrier function; tight junctions
Keratoconus: Fleischer ring (iron in epithelium), Vogt striae (stress lines in stroma), Munson sign
Drug penetration: lipophilic → through epithelium; hydrophilic → through stroma; biphasic drugs best
Aqueous Humor
Production: ciliary epithelium (active secretion 75% + ultrafiltration + diffusion)
Flow: posterior chamber → pupil → anterior chamber → trabecular meshwork → Schlemm canal → episcleral veins
Uveoscleral (unconventional) outflow: ~10-15% via ciliary muscle interstitium
Blood-aqueous barrier: tight junctions of ciliary epithelium + iris vessels
Composition: low protein, high ascorbate, glutathione
IOP normal: 10-21 mmHg; Goldmann tonometry = gold standard
Iris & Ciliary Body
Sphincter: circular muscle, parasympathetic (CN III), miosis; blocked by atropine
Dilator: radial muscle, sympathetic (superior cervical ganglion), mydriasis
Horner syndrome: sympathetic lesion → ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis; 1st/2nd/3rd order neuron
Ciliary muscle contraction (PS) → accommodation (lens rounds)
Lens
Metabolism: primarily anaerobic glycolysis; hexose monophosphate shunt
Sorbitol pathway: in diabetes, glucose → sorbitol (aldose reductase) → osmotic damage → cortical cataract
Cataract types: nuclear (brunescent, myopic shift), cortical (spoke-like), posterior subcapsular (steroids, radiation, DM)
Crystallins: most abundant lens proteins; α-crystallin = chaperone; β/γ = structural
Vitreous
Collagen type II + hyaluronan gel; liquefaction with age → lacunae → PVD
PVD: floaters + flashes; 10-15% risk of retinal tear → must dilate and examine
Vitreous as angiogenesis inhibitor: PEDF from RPE suppresses neovascularization
Enzymatic vitreolysis: ocriplasmin (microplasmin) cleaves fibronectin/laminin at vitreoretinal interface
Retina
Phototransduction (rods): photon → rhodopsin → transducin → PDE → ↓cGMP → channel closes → hyperpolarization → signal to ON bipolar cells
Visual cycle: all-trans retinal → RPE → 11-cis retinal → rhodopsin; requires vitamin A
Trivariant color vision: S (blue, ~420nm), M (green, ~530nm), L (red, ~560nm) cones
Retinal cells: photoreceptors, bipolar, ganglion, horizontal, amacrine, Müller glia, astrocytes
ERG: a-wave (photoreceptors), b-wave (bipolar/Müller cells)
Major diseases: AMD, diabetic retinopathy, retinal detachment, RP
RPE
Vitamin A regeneration: isomerizes all-trans to 11-cis retinal for visual cycle
Phagocytosis: daily phagocytosis of ~10% outer segment discs
Transport: nutrients from choriocapillaris → retina; waste in reverse
Secretion: VEGF (basal) + PEDF (apical) for choroidal homeostasis
AMD: drusen accumulate in Bruch membrane; dry (geographic atrophy) vs wet (choroidal neovascularization)
Oxidative Stress
ROS sources: light, mitochondria, NADPH oxidase, xanthine oxidase
Antioxidants: vitamin C (ascorbate), vitamin E, glutathione/GPx, SOD, catalase, carotenoids (lutein, zeaxanthin)
Retina most vulnerable: high O₂ consumption, high light exposure, high PUFA content
Implicated in: AMD (AREDS vitamins ↓risk by 25%), cataract, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy
Pharmacology
Drug Delivery Principles
Topical eyedrops: <5% bioavailability; nasolacrimal drainage = main loss route (also systemic absorption risk)
Punctal occlusion: improves topical bioavailability + reduces systemic absorption
Intravitreal injection: direct to target; avoids systemic exposure; risk of endophthalmitis
Sustained-release: biodegradable implants (Ozurdex), non-biodegradable (Iluvien)
Prodrugs: inactive until converted in eye (e.g., latanoprost → latanoprost acid)
Glaucoma Drugs
Prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost, bimatoprost): ↑uveoscleral outflow; 1st line; SEs: iris darkening, lash growth, periorbital fat atrophy
Beta blockers (timolol, betaxolol): ↓aqueous production; CI: asthma, heart block; betaxolol = selective β1
CAIs (dorzolamide topical; acetazolamide systemic): ↓aqueous production; SE: sulfa allergy, metabolic acidosis (systemic)
Alpha-2 agonists (brimonidine): ↓production + ↑uveoscleral outflow; also neuroprotective in study
ROCK inhibitors (netarsudil): ↑trabecular outflow + ↓aqueous production; conjunctival hyperemia SE
Nitric oxide donors (latanoprostene bunod): ↑trabecular + uveoscleral outflow
Miotics (pilocarpine): ↑trabecular outflow via ciliary contraction; rarely used now
Autonomic Drugs
Muscarinic agonists (pilocarpine): miosis, accommodation; used in glaucoma, reversal of dilation
Muscarinic antagonists (atropine, cyclopentolate, tropicamide): cycloplegia + mydriasis; cyclopentolate = refraction
Alpha agonists (phenylephrine): mydriasis only (no cycloplegia); nasal decongestant effect
Anticholinesterases (echothiophate): irreversible; rarely used; cataract risk with long-term use
Anti-inflammatory Drugs
Corticosteroids: prednisolone, dexamethasone, loteprednol (soft steroid, less IOP rise)
Steroid SEs: IOP elevation (steroid responders ~30%), posterior subcapsular cataract, delayed wound healing, reactivation of herpes
NSAIDs (ketorolac, nepafenac): inhibit COX; post-op inflammation, CME prevention, allergy
Anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept, faricimab): intravitreal; wet AMD, DME, CRVO
Anti-Infectives
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, moxifloxacin, besifloxacin): broad-spectrum; 4th gen best gram-positive coverage
Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin): gram-negative; retinal toxicity risk intravitreally
Vancomycin: gram-positive; endophthalmitis (intravitreal)
Ceftazidime: gram-negative; endophthalmitis (intravitreal)
Antifungals: natamycin (topical fungal keratitis), voriconazole, amphotericin B
Antivirals: acyclovir/ganciclovir (HSV/VZV/CMV); foscarnet (resistant CMV)
Other Important Drugs
Local anesthetics: proparacaine/tetracaine (topical); lidocaine/bupivacaine (injection); block Na+ channels
Osmotic agents (mannitol IV, glycerin oral): acute IOP lowering in angle-closure attack
Botulinum toxin: treats blepharospasm, hemifacial spasm, strabismus (weakens EOM)
OVDs (ophthalmic viscosurgical devices): sodium hyaluronante, HPMC; protect endothelium during surgery
Dry eye: artificial tears, cyclosporine A (Restasis), lifitegrast (Xiidra), punctal plugs
Imaging
CT Scan
Best for: bony orbit, foreign bodies (metallic), fractures, calcifications, sinus disease
Disadvantages: radiation, poor soft tissue contrast vs MRI
Use: trauma, thyroid eye disease (EOM enlargement), orbital cellulitis
MRI
Best for: optic nerve, intracranial disease, soft tissue, demyelination (MS/optic neuritis)
T1: fat bright; T2: water bright (edema, fluid)
Gadolinium: enhances areas with blood-brain barrier breakdown
Disadvantages: no metallic implants, expensive, slow, contraindicated in pacemakers
Use: optic neuritis, chiasmal lesions, cavernous sinus, orbital tumors
Ultrasonography
A-scan: 1D; measures axial length (biometry for IOL power); spikes = tissue interfaces
B-scan: 2D cross-sectional; used with opaque media (cataract, vitreous hemorrhage)
B-scan indications: retinal detachment (high reflective membrane), vitreous hemorrhage, intraocular tumor, PVD, choroidal detachment
Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM): high frequency (50 MHz); images anterior segment, angle, ciliary body in detail
Advantage: no radiation; safe in pregnancy; bedside capable
When to Order
Proptosis: CT first (bony detail) then MRI if soft tissue/ON involvement suspected
Optic neuritis: MRI brain + orbits with gadolinium (MS workup)
Trauma: CT orbits (r/o fracture, foreign body)
Opaque media: B-scan ultrasound
Biometry: A-scan for IOL calculation
Digital Ophthalmology
Data & Standards
EHR (Electronic Health Records): structured data entry; DICOM for imaging
Big Data: aggregated patient data for research; registry-based studies
Teleophthalmology
Remote screening: diabetic retinopathy, ROP, glaucoma screening in underserved areas
Store-and-forward vs synchronous models
Artificial Intelligence
Machine learning: trains on labeled data (supervised); deep learning uses CNNs for image classification
FDA-approved AI: IDx-DR (autonomous DR grading), EyeArt
Applications: AMD staging, glaucoma detection, ROP, retinal vessel analysis
Bias: training data must be diverse; avoid underrepresentation of demographics
Large Language Models (LLMs): text-based AI (e.g., GPT/Claude); clinical decision support, patient communication, documentation
Top 30 Exam Facts
LR6 SO4 All III
5 corneal layers: Epithelium, Bowman, Stroma, Descemet, Endothelium
Corneal endothelium does NOT regenerate
Aqueous pathway: ciliary body → posterior chamber → pupil → anterior chamber → trabecular meshwork → Schlemm canal → episcleral veins
JCT (juxtacanalicular) = main site of aqueous resistance
Rods: night vision/periphery; Cones: color/central (fovea)
Fovea: only cones, highest acuity; Macula: highest cone density region
Afferent pupil: CN II; Efferent: CN III; RAPD = afferent defect
Bitemporal hemianopia → chiasm (pituitary adenoma)
Homonymous hemianopia → post-chiasmal lesion
Horner: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis (sympathetic loss)
Coloboma: failure of fissure closure → inferior-nasal defect
Retinoblastoma: RB1, two-hit hypothesis; leukocoria presentation
PAX6 mutation → aniridia + Wilms tumor risk (WAGR)
Tear film: lipid (top) + mucoaqueous (bottom); goblet cells = mucin
Keratoconus signs: Fleischer ring, Vogt striae, Munson sign
Cataract types: nuclear (aging/myopia), cortical (DM), PSC (steroids/radiation/DM)
Sorbitol pathway in diabetes → osmotic cortical cataract
AMD: drusen in Bruch; dry (GA) vs wet (CNV → anti-VEGF treatment)
AREDS vitamins (C, E, β-carotene, zinc, copper) → 25% risk reduction in intermediate AMD
Diabetic retinopathy: NPDR → PDR (neovascularization); treat with laser/anti-VEGF
Retinal detachment: floaters + flashes + curtain; rhegmatogenous most common
PVD: floaters + flashes → dilate to check for retinal tear
Prostaglandins (latanoprost): ↑uveoscleral outflow; 1st-line glaucoma
Beta blockers (timolol): ↓aqueous production; beware asthma/COPD
Steroids SEs: IOP rise + PSC cataract + herpes reactivation
Anti-VEGF (intravitreal): wet AMD + DME + CRVO
CT = bone/trauma; MRI = nerve/soft tissue; U/S = opaque media
A-scan = axial length (biometry); B-scan = 2D vitreoretinal imaging
Mitochondrial inheritance (maternal): LHON, CPEO, MELAS, NARP
